examples.title: Example 2: Substitution with Both Variables on One Side

value.text: Write your solution as an ordered pair
The sum of two numbers is 20. One number is twice the other. Find both numbers.
A movie ticket costs $5 less than a concert ticket. The total cost of one of each is $55. Find the price of each ticket.
The perimeter of a rectangle is 48 cm. The length is 4 cm more than twice the width. Find the dimensions.
A taxi charges $4 plus $2 per mile. Another service charges $10 plus $1.50 per mile. After how many miles do the costs equal each other?
The sum of the digits of a two-digit number is 12. The number is 18 more than twice the tens digit. Find the number.

Multiply one or both equations if coefficients need adjustment.

Use elimination when variables are already aligned or easy to match.

Always check the solution in both equations.
The sum of two numbers is 20. Their difference is 6. Find the two numbers.
A rectangle has a perimeter of 50 cm. The length is 5 cm more than the width. Find its dimensions.
The sum of the ages of a father and his son is 60. The father is 24 years older than his son. Find their ages.
A movie ticket and a snack together cost $22. Two movie tickets and one snack cost $35. Find the price of each item.
The total value of 5-dollar and 10-dollar bills is $120. There are 16 bills in all. How many of each type are there?

unitTitle: Unit 2: Systems of Linear Equations and Inequalities
topicTitle: Topic 3: Interpreting Intersections and Solution Types

value.text: A system of two linear equations in two variables can have exactly one solution, no solution, or infinitely many solutions.
: lines intersect at exactly one point. Slopes are different.
: lines are parallel and distinct. Slopes are equal, intercepts differ.
: lines are the same line. Both slope and intercept match after simplification.
The panels below show what each case actually looks like on a graph.

conceptSummary.value:
\begin{aligned}
\text{If } &a_1x + b_1y = c_1,\quad a_2x + b_2y = c_2,\\
\text{compare ratios } &\frac{a_1}{a_2},\ \frac{b_1}{b_2},\ \frac{c_1}{c_2}.\\
&\text{One solution if ratios of }a\text{ and }b\text{ are not equal.}\\
&\text{No solution if } \frac{a_1}{a_2}=\frac{b_1}{b_2}\neq \frac{c_1}{c_2}.\\
&\text{Infinitely many if } \frac{a_1}{a_2}=\frac{b_1}{b_2}=\frac{c_1}{c_2}.
\end{aligned}

practiceParts.partTitle: Part D: Word Problems and Interpretation

value.text: A gym charges $30 per month plus $5 per class. Another gym charges $10 per month plus $8 per class. At what number of classes will the cost be the same, and what will the cost be?
One phone plan charges $50 plus $0.10 per minute, while another charges $20 plus $0.25 per minute. Find the number of minutes where both cost the same and what the total cost is.

Two taxi services charge as follows:  
    Service A: $3 plus $1.50 per mile  
    Service B: $6 plus $1.00 per mile  
    After how many miles will both cost the same?
The total cost of 3 pens and 2 notebooks is $11. The total cost of 6 pens and 4 notebooks is $22. How many solutions does this system have, and why?
), so it gives no new information.

solution.value: \boxed{\text{Infinitely many solutions, since the equations describe the same line}}

value.text: Two linear equations represent the same budget line in an economics problem. What kind of system is this, and what does that mean about the feasible combinations?
Two equations for the same line describe one system with
; every point on that line is a feasible combination, since the second equation adds no new constraint.

unitTitle: Unit 2: Systems of Linear Equations and Inequalities
topicTitle: Topic 4: Graphing Systems of Inequalities and Solution Regions

value.text: A linear inequality in two variables describes a half plane. Its boundary is the corresponding line.

conceptSummary.value: ax + by \le c,\quad ax + by < c,\quad ax + by \ge c,\quad ax + by > c.

unitTitle: Unit 2: Systems of Linear Equations and Inequalities
topicTitle: Topic 5: Modeling Real World Problems with Systems

value.text: Many word problems translate to two linear equations in two variables. Typical contexts include:
Cost and revenue with a fixed fee plus a per unit rate.
Mixtures that combine amounts and concentrations.
Motion with rate, time, and distance.
Write equations from relationships in the text.
Solve the system by substitution or elimination.
State the answer with units and interpret the coordinates.
Check that values are realistic for the context.
Translate fixed fee and per unit into
For mixtures, write one equation for total amount and one for total of the substance.
and align times or distances across objects.
Decide substitution or elimination based on the simplest path.

examples.title: Example 1: Cost and Revenue Break Even

value.text: A gym offers Plan A with a $40 sign up fee plus $15 per class, and Plan B with a $10 sign up fee plus $20 per class. For how many classes do they cost the same and what is that common cost?
At a fundraiser, student tickets cost $6 and adult tickets cost $10. In total 120 tickets were sold for $960. How many of each were sold?
: multiply the first by 6 and subtract.
How many liters of a 30 percent acid solution must be mixed with a 10 percent acid solution to get 20 liters of a 25 percent solution?
15 L of 30 percent with 5 L of 10 percent.
Two cyclists start 45 miles apart on a straight road and ride toward each other. One rides at 12 mph and the other at 15 mph. How long until they meet and how far did each travel?

examples.title: Example 5: Two trains with different departure times

value.text: Train A leaves a station at 8:00 a.m. at 50 mph. Train B leaves the same station on the same track at 9:00 a.m. at 70 mph in the same direction. At what clock time does Train B catch Train A?

be hours after 9:00 a.m.  
Distances from station at time

after 9:00 a.m.:  
Train A time is
Define variables first and attach units.
Align equations to the structure of the context. For mixtures use amount and substance equations. For motion use distance equals rate times time.
Solve, interpret, and check that answers match the language of the question.

practiceParts.partTitle: Part A: Cost and Revenue Models

value.text: A streaming plan costs $5 per month plus $2 per movie. Another plan costs $9 per month plus $1 per movie. For how many movies per month do the two plans cost the same, and what is the cost at that point?
A club charges a $20 entry fee plus $4 per visit, while another gym charges a $10 entry fee plus $5 per visit. Find the break-even number of visits.
A ride-sharing app charges $3 plus $1.25 per mile. Another service charges $6 plus $1.00 per mile. Find the number of miles at which the cost is the same.
A phone plan costs $50 per month plus $0.10 per text message. Another plan costs $25 per month plus $0.25 per text message. Find the break-even number of texts and the total cost.
. Find the break-even number of units and the revenue at that point.

practiceParts.partTitle: Part B: Ticket and Sales Problems

value.text: Concert tickets cost $25 for adults and $15 for students. 300 tickets were sold for $6,000. How many of each type were sold?
A movie theater sold 180 tickets for a total of $1,260. Adult tickets cost $8 and child tickets cost $6. Find the number of adult and child tickets sold.
A zoo charges $12 for adults and $8 for children. On one day, 250 people entered and the total collected was $2,460. Find how many adults and children attended.
A school sold 500 tickets to a fundraiser. Student tickets cost $4 and teacher tickets cost $6, raising $2,400 total. Find the number of each sold.
A concert sold 800 tickets for $9,200 total. Regular tickets were $10 and VIP tickets were $20. How many of each were sold?
How many liters of a 40 percent sugar solution must be mixed with a 20 percent sugar solution to make 30 liters of a 30 percent solution?
A jeweler combines gold that is 60 percent pure with gold that is 90 percent pure to create 100 grams of 75 percent pure gold. How many grams of each type does the jeweler use?
A chemist mixes a 10 percent acid solution with a 50 percent acid solution to get 40 liters of a 25 percent solution. Find how many liters of each solution are needed.
A 20 percent salt solution is mixed with a 50 percent solution to obtain 100 grams of a 30 percent solution. How many grams of each solution should be used?
A nutritionist wants to mix peanuts costing $3 per pound with almonds costing $7 per pound to make 20 pounds of a mixture costing $5 per pound. How many pounds of each are used?
Two cars start 300 miles apart and drive toward each other. One travels at 60 mph and the other at 50 mph. How long until they meet, and how far does each travel?
Two cyclists leave the same point. One rides east at 12 mph and the other south at 16 mph. How long until they are 100 miles apart? (Hint: use Pythagoras.)
A plane travels 400 miles with a tailwind and 400 miles against it. The plane’s airspeed is 200 mph and the wind speed is
. The total time is 4.2 hours. Find
A motorboat travels 24 miles downstream in 2 hours and the same distance upstream in 3 hours. Find the speed of the boat in still water and the speed of the current.
Two trains leave different cities 250 miles apart and travel toward each other. The faster train’s speed is 10 mph greater than the slower one. They meet in 2.5 hours. Find both speeds.
A school carnival made $540 from 150 tickets. Child tickets were $3 and adult tickets were $5. How many of each were sold?
The sum of two numbers is 24. One number is twice the other. Find both numbers using a system of equations.
The total perimeter of two rectangles is 80. One rectangle has length twice its width; the other has equal sides (a square). Their perimeters are equal. Find the dimensions of both.
A company rents trucks and vans. Each truck costs $90 per day, each van $60 per day. If 8 vehicles are rented for $600, how many are trucks and how many are vans?
A coffee shop blends coffee beans costing $8 per pound with beans costing $12 per pound to make 40 pounds of a blend worth $10 per pound. How many pounds of each are needed?
